splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether they can be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be finalized by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing plans to use more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the release term does not run during the period of absconding.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling the supervised release term when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: There is a longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any previously presented claim — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule. The answer determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing a categorical bar in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing under § 2255, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. Some circuits allow broad candidate standing based on competitive injury, while others require a more concrete showing of harm. The resolution affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes or change the rules of the electoral process.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge state election procedures absent a concrete and particularized injury beyond generalized competitive concerns.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in criminal contexts. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while others (including the Third Circuit) have held that agency deference trumps lenity. The split has deepened after Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity, as a centuries-old interpretive canon, takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference prevails over the rule of lenity, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous sentencing provisions when supported by authoritative commentary.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. The split affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums and implicates liberty interests and sentencing uniformity.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three required to disqualify)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive (any one criterion disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid and binding interpretation under Stinson and Kisor. Some circuits defer to the commentary; others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of the guideline entitled to deference under Stinson and Kisor, and thus conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly adds to rather than interprets the guideline, thus inchoate offenses do not qualify.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences — as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits allow it; others categorically bar it. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has itself generated further circuit disagreement.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, may be considered as part of or as a standalone extraordinary and compelling reason for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law cannot constitute or contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as allowing it would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held the appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that have held it belongs to the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity belong to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad scope of § 541(a) and the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtors
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtors, consistent with the Bankruptcy Code's structure, purpose, and legislative history of § 348(f).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule criminalizes substances not listed under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) is limited to substances controlled under federal law or also encompasses substances controlled only under state law. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal limitation
positions.summary: Holds that an 'offense under state law' qualifying as a controlled substance offense is not limited to substances on the federal schedules; there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal schedule controls — must match CSA
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, applying a categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a federal prisoner challenging the conditions or terms of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring a civil rights action (e.g., Bivens or § 1983).
splits.description: Since Preiser v. Rodriguez, circuits have disagreed about whether challenges to the conditions or terms of confinement — as distinct from the fact or duration of confinement — must be brought as habeas petitions or may be pursued as civil rights claims. This longstanding split affects federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions on matters like prerelease custody transfers under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the terms or conditions of confinement, including prerelease custody placement, must be brought under habeas corpus statutes.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights actions (Bivens or § 1983) to challenge conditions of confinement, with habeas reserved for challenges to the fact or duration of confinement.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to appeals from orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, though filed in a criminal case, is a civil matter subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline or a criminal matter subject to the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and therefore the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises in and is part of the criminal case, so the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for violations of supervised release when revoking and resentencing, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to the purposes outlined in 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may impose revocation sentences that expressly punish defendants for their supervised release violations. The Sixth Circuit allows punishment as a permissible purpose, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split with circuits that hold revocation sentences must focus on deterrence, public protection, and rehabilitation — not punishment.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for violating conditions of supervised release.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences under § 3583(e) must be based on the factors in § 3553(a) that Congress specified, which exclude 'just punishment' — the purpose listed in § 3553(a)(2)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of liability and available remedies under applicable federal law?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards governing discrimination claims in hospitality settings. The grant suggests the Second Circuit's framework conflicts with approaches in other circuits, and the resolution could impact hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit's approach
positions.summary: Applied legal standards for discrimination or unfair treatment claims in hospitality settings that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled or conflicting to warrant review.

positions.label: Conflicting approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishments, creating the conflict that prompted the Supreme Court's grant of certiorari.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Related precedent on public accommodation discrimination standards

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as an order effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether orders denying motions to seal documents containing private medical information qualify for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held such orders are appealable, recognizing medical privacy as a value of 'high order,' while other circuits have been more restrictive about extending the collateral order doctrine to sealing disputes.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because maintaining medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because alternative means exist to protect privacy interests, and extending immediate appealability would swallow the final judgment rule.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker arranges transportation that results in injury. The issue affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's services.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not laws related to broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether legal errors or unfairness in a conviction — the type of claims typically raised in habeas petitions under Section 2255 — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration, while others permit it in certain circumstances.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider claims related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a substitute for habeas relief and courts may not consider legal challenges to the validity of a conviction as extraordinary and compelling reasons.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
